coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of a sponge-like Z-scheme N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with enhanced photocatalytic and antimicrobial activity under visible/NIR light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Yanning
Li, Xinyang
Zhang, Hengli
Huang, Renliang
Qi, Wei
Su, Rongxin
He, Zhimin
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional photocatalytic surfaces for pollutant degradation and antimicrobial application are often in high demand, however they confront many challenges in charge transfer and light capture ability. In this work, a sponge-like N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was constructed via hydrothermal technique aiming to solve above problems. As a result, the ternary film showed enhanced photocatalytic efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light, in which 85.8% and 44.6% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) were degraded after 240 min irradiation with visible and NIR light, respectively. Moreover, the composite film effectively realized photocatalytic sterilization of gram-positive B. subtilis and gram-negative E. coli under visible light irradiation. The bacterial colony decreased significantly from 7.56-log to 1-log cfu/mL after adding the ternary film within 1.5 h. The enhanced photocatalytic efficiency was closely related to both introduction of surface-functional N,S-CQDs and the construction of N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 Z-scheme system, in which the transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers and the light absorption property were significantly improved. We consider that the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film is promising for the degradation of refractory pollutants and antimicrobial application under visible/NIR light irradiation. The relatively convenient recycling property and excellent photocatalytic performance of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film are beneficial for industrial applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional photocatalytic surfaces for pollutant degradation and antimicrobial application are often in high demand, however they confront many challenges in charge transfer and light capture ability. In this work, a sponge-like N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was constructed via hydrothermal technique aiming to solve above problems. As a result, the ternary film showed enhanced photocatalytic efficiency under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light, in which 85.8% and 44.6% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) were degraded after 240 min irradiation with visible and NIR light, respectively. Moreover, the composite film effectively realized photocatalytic sterilization of gram-positive B. subtilis and gram-negative E. coli under visible light irradiation. The bacterial colony decreased significantly from 7.56-log to 1-log cfu/mL after adding the ternary film within 1.5 h. The enhanced photocatalytic efficiency was closely related to both introduction of surface-functional N,S-CQDs and the construction of N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 Z-scheme system, in which the transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers and the light absorption property were significantly improved. We consider that the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film is promising for the degradation of refractory pollutants and antimicrobial application under visible/NIR light irradiation. The relatively convenient recycling property and excellent photocatalytic performance of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film are beneficial for industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of new manufacturing industries, the demands for multifunctional surfaces of pollutant degradation and antimicrobial function have increased in various fields, including building materials, automotive interiors, and medical facilities (Chahkandi and Zargazi, 2019; He et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019). As a new environmentally friendly technique, photocatalytic technique is essential due to its simple and high-efficiency performances (Kwon et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017). Although various powdery photocatalysts with good performance have been constructed so far, there are still apparent disadvantages in their practical applications, such as easy aggregation, low dispersion, poor recycling, and weak light response. (Sun et al., 2017a; Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop new strategies for the construction of multifunctional photocatalytic films.

Photocatalytic films growing along different substrates are often constructed by various nanostructured arrays, including nanoparticles, nanorods, nanotubes, nanosheets. (Peeters et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2017b; Zargazi and Entezari, 2019). After regular construction, their active surfaces become larger and are beneficial for photocatalytic recycling. To date, there have been various methods for film building, such as dip-coating, spin-coating, electrochemical, sol-gel, and hydrothermal methods. For example, Trabelsi et al. (2020) constructed a kind of TiO2:Er3+-Yb3+ film via the spin-coating method. Although the nanoparticles possessed an up conversion luminescence response after dispersion and spin deposition, the nanoparticles were unstable and might partly fall down from substrates. Moreover, Pérez-González and Tomás (2021) combined the dip-coating method with the sol-gel process for the construction of a Ag-loaded (TiO2)0.95-(ZnO)0.05 thin film. In general, the process reinforced the connection between materials and substrates. However, two hours were acquired for ~80% methylene blue removal under UV irradiation, i.e., the system simultaneously faced time-consuming and light-restrictive problems. It is important to reinforce the bonding between photocatalysts and the substrates during their growth (e.g., using hydrothermal or solvothermal methods etc.). According to the above phenomenon, some intricate factors influenced the photocatalytic efficiency of films (such as crystal shape, stability, catalytic surfaces) that further controlled their assembly, like light absorption and electron transfer abilities (Padmanabhan and John, 2020). Herein, the main challenge is constructing photocatalytic films via highly effective means to improve their photocatalytic efficiency.

In general, photocatalysts can be divided into two types of traditional and new-type materials, which possess respective advantages. The traditional metal oxides often have low-cost, highly productive, and simple synthetic properties (Ruiz-Gomez et al., 2018), while the new-type materials are equipped with special structures in which their internal photon-generated carriers are separated effectively (Chen et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020). Therefore, it was possible to combine them for the construction of photocatalytic films with complex performances, such as the incorporation of TiO2/BixMoyOz (Fang et al., 2019), TiO2/g-C3N4 (Zhou et al., 2019) and ZnIn2S4/TiO2 (Liu et al., 2014b). Among these heterojunctions, TiO2/Bi2MoO6 heterojunctions showed great potential in photocatalytic reactions. Because most studies have concentrated on TiO2/Bi2MoO6 powders, it is crucial to explore new technologies for the breakthrough of relevant films with enhanced photocatalytic efficiency under visible/NIR light irradiation.

Considering the above challenges, it was crucial to explore the following aspects more deeply, including the improvement of separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, the enlargement of whole light absorption, and the existence of highly active surfaces (Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). As a result, we introduced carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that possessed multifunctional features in the system. CQDs, as special type semiconductors, present certain photoelectron transfer and upconversion luminescence properties that are promising for the construction of hybrid films (Zhou et al., 2019). In general, there have been only a few reports concerning CQDs-containing films; for example, CQDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays degraded only 35.0% of MB after visible light irradiation for 100 min (Sun et al., 2014). Another kind of TiO2-CQDs film decomposed ~77.0% of RhB after 150 min (Wang et al., 2015). On the whole, the degradation efficiencies were not ideal and needed to be improved further. The key issue is investigating the internal relationships between CQDs and the basal films, matching their linking groups effectively to improve their properties. Z-scheme system was often considered as an effective way for reducing recombination of photo-induced carriers and generating isolated photoelectrons and holes for further reactions (Kim and Yong, 2021; Xie et al., 2021). However, there were still some challenges concerning design, regulation and stability in Z-scheme films.

Herein, we designed a kind of surface-functionalized and N,S-doped CQDs with PEG groups (N,S-CQDs) (Qu et al., 2020), and combined them with Bi2MoO6 and TiO2 film for construction of a new sponge-like N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with typical Z-scheme characters. The internal connection between N,S-CQDs and Bi2MoO6 in the film was tested via various characterizations. The photodegradation performances of films were evaluated under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light by using methylene blue (MB) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) as model pollutants. Investigations of active species trapping experiments and the recyclability of hybrid films were performed. In addition, the photocatalytic antibacterial properties of films were investigated via the growth inhibition of gram-positive Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli). To illustrate its growth mechanism, another CQDs sample without the addition of ethylene glycol (b-CQDs) was introduced to the system for comparison. Finally, the growth and Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanisms of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was analyzed in detail.

Butyl titanate (TBOT), sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium chloride, and the organic pollutant methylene blue (MB) were obtained from Guangfu Chemical Co. (Tianjin, China). L -cysteine, L -glutamic acid and L -tryptophan were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), acetone, yeast powder, peptone, and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Yuanli Chemical Co. (Tianjin, China). Ciprofloxacin (CIP) was purchased from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). Agar powder, glucose, ammonium oxalate (AO), and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corp. (Shanghai, China). All of the above chemicals were of analytical grade.

FTO glass (20 * 20 mm2, square resistance: < 20 Ω) was successively pretreated with acetone, ethyl alcohol, and deionized water. Then, 15 mL of HCl (37 wt%) was mixed with 15 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was stirred for 5 min. Then, 0.8 mL of TBOT was added and stirred vigorously for 5 min. Ten milliliters of the as-prepared solution was transferred to a Teflon liner. The above FTO glass that faced down the conducting side was placed in the Teflon liner and heated to 150 °C for 6 h, then cooled to room temperature (25 °C) and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h. The as-prepared film was subsequently calcined for 1 h at 500 °C.

N,S-CQDs were synthesized according to our previous report (Qu et al., 2020). Briefly, 1.5 g of L -cysteine was dissolved in a solution containing HNO3 and ethylene glycol (EG). After stirring, the as-prepared solution was heated in a Teflon autoclave for 10 h (180 °C). After cooling to room temperature (25 °C), the undispersed solids were removed by centrifugation, and the corresponding supernatant was dialyzed to obtain the N,S-CQDs. In contrast, the CQDs samples following the above procedure, except for the addition of EG, was denoted as b-CQDs, and those after changed carbon sources of glucose (C6H12O6), L -glutamic acid (C5H9NO4) and L -tryptophan (C11H12N2O2), were denoted as Glc-CQDs, Glu-CQDs and Try-CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-pot synthesis of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para: Bi(NO3)3.5H2O (0.392 g) and Na2MoO4.2H2O (0.0824 g) were completely dissolved in 15 mL of ethylene glycol via ultrasonication. The two solutions were mixed and stirred for 30 min. Then, 10 mL of the as-prepared solution was transferred to a beaker and mixed with the N,S-CQDs after dialysis (under different mass ratios (MR) of N,S-CQDs to Bi(NO3)3). The mixture was transferred to a Teflon liner, and FTO glass with TiO2 film (derived from 2.2) was placed in the liner. Then, the system was maintained at 160 °C for 6 h, cooled to room temperature (25 °C), and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h. In contrast, the Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was prepared by the above procedure except for the addition of N,S-CQDs. The N,S-CQDs/TiO2 film was prepared by the procedure described in 2.2 after the one-pot addition of N,S-CQDs.

The photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared films were evaluated via photodegradation experiments of organic pollutants under irradiation with visible light (λ > 400 nm, 273.1 mW/cm2) and NIR light (λ > 700 nm, 109.6 mW/cm2). The typical reaction was as follows: the film was put into a photocatalytic device containing organic pollutant solution (15 mL, 2 × 10−5 M). To facilitate adsorption equilibrium, the resulting system was stirred for at least 30 min in the dark. The distance from the reactants to the lamp was kept at 20 cm. The as-prepared solution was collected from the system to detect the photodegradation efficiency, in which the concentration of CIP was detected by the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and the possible intermediates were analyzed via liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS). Specifically, the specific flow rate, column oven temperature and injection volume were 0.4 mL/min, 35 °C and 10 μL, respectively; a C18 column was used for separation of intermediates; the gradient elution solvents contained A (acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid) and B (DI water).

The environmental water samples including two kinds of surface water (from Tianjin University Lake (TJU Lake) and Tianjin Lake) and a type of underground water (in Tianjin, China) were obtained before experiments, in which the water samples were filtered via microporous membranes (50 mm, 0.45 µm) for three times to remove suspended particulate matters thoroughly. The photodegradation reactions were through the same procedure as described in part 2.5.

The antibacterial activities of the films were determined against gram-positive B. subtilis and gram-negative E. coli under visible light irradiation. First, 1 g of yeast powder, 2 g of peptone, 2 g of sodium chloride, 2 g of agar powder, and 200 mL of deionized water were mixed as the bacterial culture medium. All the materials, culture dishes, and photocatalytic reactors were sterilized for 20 min at 120 °C in a high-handed sterilization pan before the experiments. The sterilization reaction was as follows: the film was put into the bacterial-cell solution (15 mL), and then the resulting system was stirred for at least 30 min in the dark to facilitate adsorption equilibrium before the reaction. After stirring at certain intervals, an aliquot of bacterial suspension was immediately diluted and spread onto the bacterial culture medium. The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the number of viable cells was determined by the plate count method. Except for the as-prepared films, the blank control represented the photocatalytic antimicrobial process without the addition of any films under visible light irradiation.

The configurations of the samples were evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (FESEM, S4800, Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses (Talos F200X G2, FEI, America). The photodegradation efficiencies of pollutants were analyzed by a Pgeneral TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Purkinje Inc., Beijing, China) equipped with quartz cells. The elemental analyses were conducted with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) equipped in the S-4800 FESEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) performing with a PHI1600 ESCA System (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA). Nitrogen adsorption experiments were performed at − 196 °C on a Gemini VII 2390 apparatus, in which Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas were calculated based on the adsorption isotherms. The surface structures were recorded by atomic force microscopy (AFM 5500, Agilent Co., America). The contact angles of films were measured with an OCA15EC contact angle measuring device (Data Physics Instruments, Germany) equipped with SCA 202 software. The mineralization efficiency of the solution was evaluated by a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-VCPH E200 V, Shimadzu Co., Japan). The structural characterizations of the samples were performed using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D/MAX-2500, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation and an FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet 6700) via the ATR method. The diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as a 100% reflectance standard. The photoluminescence (PL) investigation was executed using a Fluorolog3 photoluminescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, America) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The energy band values of films were detected by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, America) at a bias voltage of − 5 eV. The fluorescence lifetime was recorded via a transient photoluminescence decay spectra (Edinburgh PLS980 spectrometer) under an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of composite films were obtained on a Bruker model ESR spectrometer (JES-FA300), in which the 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was employed as spin-trapping agents of·OH and·O2 - species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic process of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film is shown in Scheme 1. First, FTO glass was employed as a substrate for the construction of TiO2 nanorod arrays. Then, N,S-CQDs were directly embedded into the Bi2MoO6 precursor solution. After the one-pot hydrothermal process, a N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6 upper film was stacked on TiO2 nanorod arrays for construction of a novel ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film. The film showed a special sponge-like structure and was applied in pollutant photodegradation and photocatalytic antibacterial researches.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the bottom of the TiO2 film presented typical nanorod arrays with an average thickness of ~1.21 µm. After the following modification of Bi2MoO6, distinct nanosheet arrays appeared on the top layer with an increasing thickness of ~0.20 µm (Fig. 1c). By comparison, the composite N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film transformed to a novel sponge-like morphology and maintained a similar thickness of ~1.42 µm as the Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film under the influence of N,S-CQDs (Fig. 1e). It is noted that the connection between Bi2MoO6 and TiO2 was via physical loading after hydrothermal reaction. The surface-functionalized N,S-CQDs with PEG groups (Qu et al., 2020) linked to Bi2MoO6 via both absorption effect (i.e., connection of PEG on Bi2MoO6 nuclei (Dumrongrojthanath et al., 2014)) and the coordinated complexation (i.e., coordination of PEG to the metal ions (Wang et al., 2013)) during formation of Bi2MoO6 through a one-pot hydrothermal method. Moreover, the elemental distribution of the ternary film is shown in Fig. S1. Generally, except for Ti, O, Mo, and Bi, N and S were also detected in the EDS spectra, mainly derived from N,S-CQDs. In addition, the structure of the N,S-CQDs/TiO2 film was investigated for comparison with other films. According to Fig. S2, a structure of nanorod arrays resembling TiO2 film was shown in the image after introduction of N,S-CQDs inside the film, which indicated that the combination of TiO2 and N,S-CQDs did not have much effect on the integral configuration of the TiO2/N,S-CQDs film.

TEM and HRTEM images of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film are shown in Fig. 2. Some evident nanorods with interplanar spacings of ~0.352 nm were shown in the composite film, representing the existence of TiO2 nanorods. After partial amplification, some globular structures with ~4 nm were distributed on the nanosheets, which were recognized as typical N,S-CQDs with a lattice fringe of ~0.32 nm. Also, some dispersed Bi2MoO6 nanosheets with evident interplanar spacings of ~0.314 nm were found in the film. Therefore, the above results demonstrated the embeddedness of N,S-CQDs in the hybrid film at the microlevel. The superficial property of the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was further characterized and compared to TiO2 film via AFM images. Fig. S3 showed some variations between the ternary and single films; specifically, the ternary film possessed a higher roughness value of ~62.68 nm than that of the single TiO2 film (~50.92 nm). Moreover, the contact angles of the as-prepared films were investigated, as shown in Fig. S4. In comparison to TiO2 film, the contact angles of other polynary films decreased to a certain degree. The N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film presented a hydrophilic surface with a contact angle of ~16.1°.

Furthermore, the XRD patterns of different films were researched and compared. As shown in Fig. 2g, except for a few diffraction peaks derived from FTO (Zuo et al., 2019), the peaks in the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film corresponded to various lattice planes in TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 materials. Specifically, the peaks at 36.78°, 41.80°, 55.05° and 70.43° were indexed to the (101), (111), (211) and (112) planes, respectively, which corresponded to typical tetragonal rutile TiO2 (JCPDS No. 88-1175) (Liu et al., 2014b). Moreover, the other characteristic peaks of 2θ = 28.20°, 33.20°, 47.25°, and 55.30° were recognized as the (131), (200), (062), and (331) planes, respectively, which represented the orthorhombic phase of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 76-2388) (Cai et al., 2017). Additionally, no pronounced peak concerning N,S-CQDs was detected in the patterns, possibly attributed to the low amount of N,S-CQDs in the film (Di et al., 2015).

Moreover, the internal connection between N,S-CQDs and Bi2MoO6 was investigated via FITR spectra. From Fig. 2h, the bands at 3419 and 1660 cm−1 in the spectrum of Bi2MoO6@TiO2 mainly represented the O-H stretching and bending vibrations in TiO2 (Zhou et al., 2019). The typical Mo-O and Bi-O stretching vibrations in Bi2MoO6 were recognized within absorption ranges of ~700–900 cm−1 (Di et al., 2015; Samanta et al., 2017), all presented in the spectra of Bi2MoO6@TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 films. Additionally, there were some differences in the above films; for instance, the broadband intensity at ~1254 cm−1 of the ternary film was higher than that of the binary film, which was possibly attributed to the increased C-O vibrations in N,S-CQDs. Moreover, some new bands presented near 1621 and 1615 cm−1 were likely derived from the -CO groups and N-H stretching vibration peaks in N,S-CQDs (Qu et al., 2020). Furthermore, the bands at 2958 and 2924 cm−1 indicated the presence of alkyl chain groups (containing -CH2- and -CH3) in the composite films. Accordingly, the above results further demonstrated the existence of embedded N,S-CQDs in the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film.

To further study the internal structure of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film, XPS spectra were obtained. From Fig. 3a, seven main elements, including Ti 2p, Bi 4f, Mo 3d, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p, were detected in the full spectrum, in which several important elements were under the peak fitting analysis. At first, there were two main fitted peaks containing 456.8 and 464.1 eV in Ti 2p that corresponded to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively. Moreover, Bi 4f could be fitted with two peaks at 158.1 and 163.3 eV and severally attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 binding energies that potentially matched the Bi3+ state in the film (Zuo et al., 2019). The Mo 3d was fitted at 231.8 and 234.5 eV peaks that corresponded to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, indicating the existence of Mo6+ in MoO4 2- (Cai et al., 2017). In addition, the relevant data of C 1s and O 1s were further analyzed, in which C 1s was fitted with three peaks at 283.9, 285.2, and 287.9 eV and attributed to the C-H, C-N, and CO bonds in some groups of N,S-CQDs, respectively (Qu et al., 2020). In O 1s, the structure of Mo-O/Bi-O bonds was fitted at 528.5 eV, and the other peak at 529.9 eV might represent Ti-O/O-H oxygens in the composite film (Samanta et al., 2017). Moreover, the fitted peaks at 399.4, 400.2 eV in N 1s mainly attributed to the C-N and N-H bonds, respectively, which indicated the existence of amine groups in N,S-CQDs (Luo and Jiang, 2019; Wang et al., 2019). In S 2p, the spectrum was fitted with two peaks at 160.2 and 165.4 eV that showed certain -S-S- and C-S/S-H bonds in the film (Lu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). According to previous reports (Shavandi et al., 2021), the L -cysteine played an important role to the formation of disulfide bonds so that the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was more stabilized after introduction of N,S-CQDs. Overall, the main elements and structures in the three ingredients of TiO2, Bi2MoO6, and N,S-CQDs of the ternary film were distinguished in detail via XPS spectra.

The light-harvesting and photoelectric properties of the as-prepared films are shown in Fig. 4. In general, the absorption intensities of polynary films were higher than that of the single TiO2 film under an extensive range containing visible and NIR light (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the intensity of the ternary film was highest among them, especially in the visible light region. The plots of (Ahν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν) were further explored via extrapolated transformation from the data in Fig. 4a. The band-gap energy (Eg) of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film decreased to ~2.74 eV, while that of the TiO2 film was ~2.96 eV (Fig. 4b). The band-gap energy of single Bi2MoO6 (~2.31 eV) and N,S-CQDs film (~1.24 eV) were also investigated as shown in Fig. S5.

From Fig. 4c, the cyclic response of the photocurrent signal switched with on-off light. In particular, a noticeable enhancement of the transient photocurrent was presented on the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film compared with other films. This result indicated that a more effective transformation of photogenerated electrons and holes was evident in the ternary film (Bai et al., 2019). Moreover, according to relevant EIS data (Fig. 4d), the arc radius on the Nyquist plot of the ternary film showed the lowest result, which further demonstrated its improvement of interfacial charge transfer efficiency.

In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the films were investigated, as shown in Fig. S6a. Overall, the highest fluorescence intensity was presented on the single TiO2 film, while that of the ternary hybrid film evidently decreased to the lowest value. Because the fluorescence is often attributed to recombination of photoelectrons and vacancies (Tian et al., 2013), the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with lower PL intensity showed more effective separation of photoinduced carriers. Moreover, from Fig. S6b, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of Bi2MoO6@TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film were obtained, typically, the average fluorescence lifetime values of above films were 1.24 and 5.38 ns, respectively. According to previous reports (Gao et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2018), the longer lifetime of excited electron was, the better separation effect of photogenerated electron and hole was in photocatalytic system. Therefore, the above results further demonstrated significant roles of N,S-CQDs in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation activity of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the photodegradation efficiencies of the above films, MB and CIP were employed as mimic organic pollutants for their reactions under visible and NIR light. From Fig. 5a, 99.8% of the initial MB was degraded after 90 min by the ternary film under visible light, while 90.6%, 84.2%, and 44.4% of MB were degraded by the Bi2MoO6@TiO2, N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and TiO2 films, respectively. For CIP, low efficiencies were presented on the single and binary films, while it was enhanced to 85.8% for the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film after visible light irradiation for 240 min (Fig. 5c).

It is worth noting that the adsorption performance of the film played a significant role during photodegradation. The adsorption efficiencies of films were further compared in detail in Fig. S7, in which the ternary film showed a higher adsorption efficiency of ~35.8% than that of others during the dark period of 30 min, which was generally stable at around 30 min. One the one hand, the results were influenced by various specific surface area of the films. Due to the difficulty of as-prepared films peeling from the substrate, a special calculation method was designed here according to previous reports (Liu et al., 2014a; Ruan et al., 2014). From Table S1, the relative S BET of N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film showed the highest value within the films that provided a reliable demonstration of its more effective adsorption of pollutant molecules than other films. One the other hand, the uniform arrays (like TiO2 nanorods and the sponge-like N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6) possessed certain interspace and pore canals on the film (Chahkandi and Zargazi, 2019; Liu et al., 2014a), which was benefitted to their enhancement of adsorption property than that of the powder materials.

Herein, we compared the efficiencies of some relevant films for the photodegradation of MB under visible light. For example, a kind of CQDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays just degraded 35.0% of the initial MB after 100 min (Sun et al., 2014); a previous ternary Au/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film degraded 68.0% of MB within 120 min (Cai et al., 2017). Additionally, other kinds of CQDs derived from glucose (C6H12O6 without N, S elements), L -glutamic acid (C5H9NO4 without S doping) and L -tryptophan (C11H12N2O2 without S doping) were employed for construction of different CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 films (which were denoted as Glc-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2, Glu-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 and Try-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2, respectively). As a result, the above films showed MB degradation efficiency of 83.9%, 74.3% and 80.3% respectively after visible light irradiation of 90 min, and severally degraded 48.3%, 44.7% and 55.9% of CIP under visible light of 240 min (Fig. S8). In contrast, the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film showed a higher degradation efficiency than that of previous films, which was attributed to its significant design techniques.

Moreover, the photodegradation dynamics of the above films were analyzed. In general, a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation was concluded by

where

is the concentration of organic pollutant after a period of time and

is the concentration at the beginning. k is a pseudo-first-order kinetic constant, and t is the irradiation time. According to the kinetic equation, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film showed a k value of 0.0623 min−1 for the photodegradation of MB under visible light. The value decreased to 0.0262, 0.0201, and 0.0065 min−1 for the Bi2MoO6@TiO2, N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and TiO2 films, respectively. Moreover, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film maintained a kinetic constant k of 0.0064 min−1 for the photodegradation of CIP under visible light irradiation.

Additionally, the photocatalytic activities of the ternary films under different mass ratios (MR) of N,S-CQDs to Bi(NO3)3 were analyzed, as shown in Fig. S9. It was found that the photocatalytic efficiency of the film enhanced gradually with increasing N,S-CQDs and reached the highest value at MR= 1.06; however, the efficiency decreased to a certain degree with sequentially increased MR. Therefore, the additional amount of N,S-CQDs should be controlled within limits. In addition, the total organic carbon (TOC) experimental results are recorded in Fig. S10. Specifically, 50.1% of CIP was mineralized by the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film after photodegradation for 240 min under visible light, which indicated certain effects on TOC removal.

Herein, the possible reaction pathways ( Fig. 6) and structural formulas (Table S2) during photodegradation of CIP were investigated via HPLC-MS measurement. According to relevant reports, we deduced three possible degradation pathways of CIP. From pathway I, a typical nucleophilic addition reaction showed in the CIP and resulted in its cleavage of benzene ring for generation of a ketone structure (m/z = 364) under the effect of superoxide free radicals (Wang et al., 2018a). In order to keep balanced, the open loop structure was transformed to a relatively stable ring structure (m/z = 285) after corresponding decarboxylation and dehydration reactions. The intermediate (m/z = 185) with smaller molecular weight was further detected after the advanced oxidation. According to pathway II, some structures of m/z = 262 and 228 were found in the system. It indicated that the CIP was direct attacked by superoxide or hydroxide free radicals and resulted in generation of the structure m/z = 262 via a series of dealkylation and deamination reactions, then the -F and -OH group were removed further (m/z = 228) (Ao et al., 2018; Che et al., 2018). Additionally, there was another possible procedure of pathway Ⅲ, the piperazine ring and -F group on CIP were initially substituted by -OH for generation of a structure (m/z = 345) under effect of hydroxide free radicals. Then the five-membered ring (m/z = 315) was formed via the opened piperazine ring, after subsequent decarboxylation reaction, a smaller structure (m/z = 271) was obtained in the system (Ao et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2018). Finally, some smaller molecules, such as CO2, H2O, etc. were likely to be acquired after deep photodegradation.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared films under NIR light were further investigated, as shown in Fig. S11. According to the results, 44.6% of CIP and 62.2% of MB were degraded by the ternary film after NIR light irradiation for 240 min. In contrast, the binary Bi2MoO6@TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 film only degraded 36.0% and 7.9% of CIP, respectively, for the same time. According to the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film showed k values of 0.0014 and 0.0030 min−1 for the photodegradation of CIP and MB under NIR light, respectively. The above result was probably attributed to the effective upconverted luminescence property of N,S-CQDs, which expanded the absorption efficiency of sunlight of the novel N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film (Qu et al., 2020).

Moreover, the environmental water samples including two types of surface water (from Tianjin University Lake (TJU Lake) and Tianjin Lake) and a kind of underground water (in Tianjin, China) were collected for tolerance detection of the ternary film. As shown in Fig. S12a, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film degraded 75.1%, 64.6% and 68.8% of CIP after visible light irradiation of 240 min in TJU Lake, Tianjin Lake and the underground water, respectively. Moreover, a dual pollutant system with both CIP and MB in TJU Lake was prepared for photodegradation under visible light, typically, 88.8% of MB and 62.9% of CIP were respectively removed after 90 and 240 min at the same time (Fig. S12b). Accordingly, the above results demonstrated obvious photocatalytic efficiency of the ternary film under actual water systems, which was potential for more applications in industry.

To analyze the photodegradation mechanism of the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film, relevant ESR spectra and active species trapping experiments are recorded in Fig. 7 and Fig. S13. As shown in Fig. 7a-b, the two significant active radicals including superoxide radical anions

From Fig. 7c-d, three different scavengers, IPA, AO, and BQ, were employed to trap active species of hydroxide free radicals ( ∙OH ), holes (h+), and superoxide radical anions (

), respectively. As a result, 93.7%, 74.4%, and 40.1% of MB were degraded by the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film in the presence of IPA, AO, and BQ, respectively. Accordingly, h+ and

The trapping experiments of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 and Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film were obtained to make comparisons with above results. From Fig. S13a, after addition of the three types of active species during photodegradation of MB, ∙OH , h+ and

As shown in Fig. S13b, the corresponding photodegradation efficiencies of CIP decreased obviously under effects of IPA, AO, and BQ. It demonstrated that the three kinds of active species all influenced on the photodegradation procedure of CIP of N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film, in which the
∙O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation recycling of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the significant recycling abilities of as-prepared films, they were employed for five cycles of MB photodegradation reactions. From Fig. 8 and Fig. S14, the degradation efficiency of Bi2MoO6@TiO2, N,S-CQDs/TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film were 69.6%, 69.1% and 96.3% respectively after 5 cycles, in which the ternary film showed an excellent recyclability surpassing most photocatalytic powders (Hao et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2017a) that was advantageous to promising applications in industry. Additionally, XRD patterns, SEM images, and XPS spectra of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film after five cycles were obtained. From Fig. S15a, the characteristic peaks of Bi2MoO6 and TiO2 were still detected in the pattern after photocatalytic cycles and were similar to the original pattern, representing the good crystal stability of the film. Additionally, a sponge-like morphology of the film remained, indicating its good structural stability (Fig. S15b).

The XPS spectra of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film before and after five cycles were further compared (Fig. S16), and the main elements under full-spectrum after five cycles are recorded in Table S3. Accordingly, the contents of the main elements decreased slightly after the reactions and showed good results. Moreover, the high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p, Bi 4f, and Mo 3d were all fitted to two main peaks after reactions and matched with those of the original peaks. For the specific spectra of C 1s and O 1s after fitting, some main C-H, C-N, Mo-O/Bi-O, and Ti-O bonds were still detected in the spectra. Additionally, the bonds of C-N, -S-S- and C-S in fitted N 1s and S 2p spectra still presented in the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film after multiple photocatalytic reactions. Therefore, the above results demonstrated that the internal structure of the ternary film was not significantly affected by the recycling experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic antibacterial abilities of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic antibacterial activities of the as-prepared films were investigated further via growth inhibition of gram-positive B. subtilis and gram-negative E. coli under visible light irradiation. From Fig. 9a, the cell density of B. subtilis only decreased partially with the TiO2 film after 3 h, while the bacterial colony decreased prominently (from 7.56-log to 1-log cfu/mL) after the addition of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film within 1.5 h, suggesting its excellent effect on B. subtilis sterilization. From Fig. 9c, the bacterial colony of E. coli decreased from 8.73-log to 4.60-log cfu/mL after an antibacterial experiment of 3 h with the ternary film, which was better than that of the single and binary films. Moreover, the specific changes in bacterial colonies at different time and dilution multiples are more clearly shown in Fig. S17.

In general, there are often three periods during photocatalytic disinfection, according to previous reports (Li et al., 2017b; Zhang et al., 2018b). In the beginning, an initial delay called the "shoulder" was obtained, and then the second period with a significant log-linear region was observed with the fast disinfection process. Finally, the deceleration called the "tail" represented the termination of the process. Correspondingly, the kinetics behaviors for the disinfection process were fitted via a modified Hom model as follows:
(2)

N
N 0
= -
k 1
[ 1 - exp (-
k 2
t
)]
k 3

where N and N 0 are the bacterial cell densities at time t and t 0, respectively. k 1, k 2, and k 3 are the kinetic constants in different periods. Overall, since the intermediate period is often the rate-determining step, the k 2 value is crucial for the whole antibacterial efficiency evaluation. The specific constants k 2 of different bacteria (derived from Fig. 9b and Fig. 9d) are recorded in Table S4. The k 2 values of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film were 0.0813 and 0.0165 min−1 for B. subtilis and E. coli, respectively, and were obviously higher than those of the TiO2, N,S-CQDs/TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 films.

Overall, the antibacterial results of gram-positive B. subtilis were better than those of the gram-negative E. coli by the films. When compared antibacterial researches of B. subtilis with other reports, the ternary film showed a better result than that of the Ag2O/TiO2 nanobelts (Jin et al., 2013) (which employed ~6 h for complete inactivation under visible light), the SmVO4/fluorine doped graphene material (Shandilya et al., 2018) (which decreased 98% of viable cells after 3 h photocatalytic reaction) and some other materials as shown in Table S5. It is found that the antibacterial efficiency of ternary film was also close to that of some Ag based-composites, which was attributed to both special ternary composition and the intrinsic sterilization property of N,S-CQDs (Ma et al., 2022; Travlou et al., 2018) that influenced on above results in the system. According to some reports, the antibacterial mechanism is mainly determined by the reactive species of photocatalytic films. After attraction by reactive species, the bacterial cells were destroyed from the initial surface to the internal structure and gradually became inactivated (Han et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021, 2018; Xia et al., 2018, 2019). Therefore, the above results further demonstrated the significance of N,S-CQDs in ternary films for improving photocatalytic activity compared to single and binary films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and growth mechanisms of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film
ce:section.ce:para:
According to above results of active species trapping experiments, except for
∙O
2
-
, ∙OH and h+ were also generated and played certain roles in the system especially in photodegradation of CIP. In order to analyze the mechanisms, the valence band maximum (VBM) and conduct band minimum (CBM) of different ingredients in N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film were obtained from ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). As shown in Fig. S18, the low (E low) and high (E high) kinetic energy cutoff could be obtained from corresponding tangent lines of UPS in different films (Jiménez-Calvo et al., 2020; Kim and Yong, 2021). The corresponding E VBM and E CBM (vs. vacuum level) were calculate by the equations.
(3)
E VBM = hν – (E high – E low)
(4)
E CBM = E VBM – E g

(in which hν is 21.2 eV of incident photon energy). Due to the absolute potential energy of normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is ~4.44 V (25 °C), the electrode potential values of different films were calculated via deducting 4.44 V from above E VBM and E CBM under the reference standard of NHE (Li et al., 2020). As a result, the conduct and valence band electrode potential of TiO2 film were − 1.05 V and 1.91 V (vs. NHE), correspondingly, the above results of Bi2MoO6 film were − 1.39 V and 0.92 V (vs. NHE), respectively (Fig. S5, Table S6).

According to the energy bands of as-prepared films, a conventional type II scheme was initially assumed in the photocatalytic reaction (as shown in Fig. 10a). However, herein the redox potential of OH–/ ∙OH (1.99 V vs. NHE) and H2O/ ∙OH (2.34 V vs. NHE) are all higher than the valence band of Bi2MoO6 so that ·OH cannot be generated under this energy level, which was also contradictory to above ESR spectra and trapping experiments of active species (in Fig. 7 and Fig. S13) (Jiang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2021). As a result, a Z-scheme procedure was deduced to the system, in which N,S-CQDs were regarded as electron transporting media for migration of charges that the electrons and holes were separated effectively (Pan et al., 2018). From Fig. 10b,
∙O
2
-
can be generated via photoelectrons in VB of Bi2MoO6, while ·OH still cannot be formed via the photogenerated holes (in the CB of TiO2). According to some reports and certainly detected ·OH in films from ESR spectra, the ·OH largely originated from reaction of
∙O
2
-
with other species (Feng et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020) (as shown in Fig. 10c). On the whole, the corresponding electrons and holes in the Z-scheme system showed a better oxidation reducibility that the photocatalytic activity of ternary film was improved in a certain degree (Huang et al., 2019; Xiang et al., 2020).

Moreover, the growth process of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was further investigated in detail. From Fig. S19, SEM images of the ternary films under different time intervals were obtained. First, the TiO2 nanorod arrays were loaded on the substrate, and then after 1.2 h, some nanosheet composites grew on the pre-existing arrays. As time went on, the nanosheets stacked together and assembled to a denser configuration. Consequently, a special sponge-like structure was present on the film after 6 h. In addition, SEM images of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 films under different mass ratios (MR) of N,S-CQDs to Bi(NO3)3 are recorded in Fig. S20. It was found that a prominent structure of nanosheet arrays was present in the image at MR= 0.15; however, with increasing MR, some aggregation structures of nanosheets were gradually enhanced. Notably, the aggregation state became tighter when MR increased to 1.35. Therefore, in contrast to the Bi2MoO6/TiO2 film with a nanosheet array structure (Tian et al., 2013), the N,S-CQDs enormously influenced the morphology of the ternary film.

According to previous literature, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is often employed as both structure-directing and binding agent for constructing Bi2MoO6 nanosheet arrays (Li et al., 2017b, 2013). Due to the special design of PEG coatings on the surfaces of N,S-CQDs (Qu et al., 2020), the superfacial groups of N,S-CQDs played an important role in formation of the hybrid film. To analyze the above phenomenon in more detail, the CQDs without modification of ethylene glycol (denoted b-CQDs) were obtained to construct the b-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film. As shown in Fig. S21a-b, slight accumulation of nanosheet structures with certain interspaces was observed in the film. Furthermore, another kind of film was constructed for a more distinct comparison with the above film. First, the TiO2 film was immersed into the N,S-CQDs solution for pretreatment for 24 h (denoted as N,S-CQDs@TiO2). Then, a new b-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@N,S-CQDs@TiO2 composite film formed via the previous methods at the N,S-CQDs@TiO2 bottom layer on the substrate. Fig. S21c-d shows an obvious, compactly stacked sponge-like structure on the new composite film that is exactly similar to the configuration of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film. Through analysis, the above results were possibly attributed to further growth of the Bi2MoO6 nanosheet arrays on the basal TiO2 layer under the influence of surface-functional N,S-CQDs rather than b-CQDs. Consequently, the special design and introduction of N,S-CQDs had a significant effect on constructing the novel film.

In general, the possible growth mechanism of the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film is summarized in Scheme 2. Under the influence of N,S-CQDs, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite initially grew along the TiO2 nanorod arrays to construct a small number of nanosheets, and then the nanosheets continuously aggregated to form a nanosheet array layer (Bu et al., 2019). Owing to the function of PEG coatings on N,S-CQDs, the nanosheets derived from Bi2MoO6 regrew directionally based on the existing arrays (Li et al., 2017b; Qu et al., 2020). Consequently, the nanosheets became denser, which resulted in the formation of a novel sponge-like film on the TiO2 arrays. According to previous reports, there were mainly two advantages of photocatalysts with sponge morphology, firstly, their larger specific surface area enhanced the absorption ability of reactant molecules on material surfaces that their interfacial photocatalytic reaction were promoted further (Zhang et al., 2018a). On the other hand, the special sponge structure was benefitted to uniform distribution of other dopants and cocatalysts (like the N,S-CQDs, Ag etc.) in their porous channels, which facilitated the transfer of photoelectrons on surfaces of photocatalysts (Liang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018b). Notably, after the construction of N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 composite, a typical Z-scheme system formed on the substrate, which facilitated the separation of photoelectrons and holes effectively (Jiang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2021). Moreover, the introduction of N,S-CQDs, on the one hand, facilitated the reception and transfer of electrons in the system, on the other hand, it benefited NIR light absorption and subsequent conversion of visible light of the composite film due to excellent upconverted luminescence property (Qu et al., 2020).

It was known that the absorbed light with long-wave band could be switched to launching light with short-wave band in the system (which is denoted as switched hv) owing to the upconverted luminescence property of N,S-CQDs for generation of e-/h+ pairs. Then O2 was employed as a main electron accepter that trapped the photoinduced e- (on CB of Bi2MoO6) to produce the active species·O2 -. The ·O2 - showed an effective oxidizability that accelerated photodegradation and photocatalytic antibacterial processes (Li et al., 2017a). Additionally, the reactive ·OH (derived from
∙O
2
-
and other species) and photogenerated h+ could also oxidize the target objects after transferring to the surface of the film (Sun et al., 2017a). The possible photodegradation mechanism is proposed as follows:
(5) N,S-CQDs + hν → switched hv
(6) Bi2MoO6/TiO2 + switched hv → Bi2MoO6/TiO2 (e- + h+)
(7) Bi2MoO6 (e-) + O2 → Bi2MoO6 + ·O2 -
(8) O2 + e-→ ·O2 - → HO2·
(9) 2HO2· → O2 + H2O2
(10) ·O2 - + H2O2 → OH- + O2 + ·OH
(11) ·O2 -+ Pollutants/Bacteria → Degraded products
(12) ·OH/h++ Pollutants/Bacteria → Degraded products

Overall, some relevant hybrid films and their photodegradation efficiencies for organic dyes under visible light are summarized in Table S7. It was found that the ternary N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film generally had better efficiency than that of the binary Bi2MoO6/TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 array films. Additionally, due to the particular upconversion fluorescence (Qu et al., 2020), electronic reception and transfer properties of N,S-CQDs, the novel film exhibited higher activity than some of the ternary hybrid films (e.g., the Au/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 arrays). According to Table S8, the efficiencies of some previous films aiming to degrade antibiotics were also analyzed. It was found that the result in this work was better than most traditional and relevant new-type composite films as a whole.

In summary, due to the challenges of relevant reports concerning the effective design of CQDs and connection with basal materials on photocatalytic films, the combination of N,S-CQDs with Bi2MoO6 initially provided a new thought matching their linking groups together. As a result, the ternary Z-scheme N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with typical sponge-like structure showed multifunctional properties on both photodegradation and photocatalytic antibacterial processes that were significant for the subsequent researches of photocatalytic films.

In summary, a sponge-like N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with multifunctional properties was successfully constructed via a hydrothermal technique, in which N,S-CQDs showed typical electron acceptor and donor functions in the Z-scheme system. Approximately 85.8% and 44.6% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) were degraded by the ternary film after 240 min irradiation with visible and NIR light, respectively, which were better than those of the single and binary films. Meanwhile, the ternary composite film showed effective photocatalytic sterilization roles containing gram-positive B. subtilis and gram-negative E. coli under visible light irradiation. According to active species trapping experiments, ESR spectra and band structure analyses, the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film was closely related to the Z-scheme system, in which its absorbed-light ranges was also enlarged effectively owing to excellent upconverted luminescence property of N,S-CQDs, especially at NIR light. As a result, the transfer efficiency of internal photoinduced carriers and the light absorption properties were all effectively improved in N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film. Considering the excellent photocatalytic efficiency and convenient recycling properties, we believe that the N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film has great environmental pollutant photodegradation and antimicrobial application potentials in industry.

Yanning Qu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinyang Li: Investigation. Hengli Zhang: Investigation. Renliang Huang: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition. Wei Qi: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Rongxin Su: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhimin He: Writing - review & editing.

A novel sponge-like Z-scheme N,S-CQDs/Bi2MoO6@TiO2 film with multifunctional photocatalytic properties for pollutant degradation and antimicrobial application was prepared after hydrothermal techniques. Owing to excellent upconverted luminescence and electron transporting advantages of N,S-CQDs, the combination of traditional photocatalyst TiO2, new-type Bi2MoO6 and N,S-CQDs made a special Z-scheme procedure come true that electrons and holes were separated effectively in the system. We expect that this finding is beneficial for more industrial applications in hazardous pollutant disposal.
